coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot modified electrodes developed for electrochemical monitoring of Daunorubicin-DNA interaction
coredata.dc:creator: Eksin, Ece
Senturk, Huseyin
Zor, Erhan
Bingol, Haluk
Erdem, Arzum
coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dot (cQD) modified disposable pencil graphite electrodes were developed for the first time in this study for electrochemical monitoring of drug-DNA interaction. The biomolecular interaction between calf thymus double stranded DNA (ctDNA) and an anthracycline antineoplastic drug, Daunorubicin (DNR) was investigated by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. For this purpose, experimental conditions, such as, the concentration of ctDNA, concentration of DNR and interaction time were optimized. Under optimum conditions, the detection limits of DNR and ctDNA were found to be 0.02 μg/mL and 0.89 μg/mL, respectively. The effect of interaction time between DNR and ctDNA was explored upon to the changes at both DNR and guanine oxidation signals. Drug-DNA interaction process was also examined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Pencil graphite electrodes
Drug-DNA interaction
Differential pulse voltammetry
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: cQD-PGE based DNA biosensor construction and drug-DNA interaction
cQD modification onto the surface of PGE
DNA immobilization onto the surface of cQD-PGEs
DNR-ctDNA interaction onto the surface of cQD-PGEs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM lateral image (A), 3D view (B) and (C) the corresponding height profile of cQD sample dispersed on a silicon surface.
The SEM micrographs for PGE (A) and cQD-PGE (B) at low and high magnification using the identical acceleration voltage as 5.0 kV.

The concept of fabrication of the cQD modified pencil graphite electrodes. Left, the sketch of the cQD modification onto the surface of PGE. cQD modification is performed by passive adsorption by dipping PGEs into the cQD solution during 1 h. Then cQD-PGEs were air dried for 10 min before electrochemical measurements. (I) Cyclic voltammograms of PGE and cQD-PGE. Supporting electrolyte solution is 0.1 M KCl containing 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. (II) Nyquist diagrams of PGE and cQD-PGE. Supporting electrolyte solution is 0.1 M KCl containing 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. Inset is the equivalent circuit model used for fitting of the impedance data.

ctDNA concentration study using both PGE and cQD-PGE. (A) DPVs representing (a) the control of PGE in ABS, the guanine oxidation signal of (b) 10, (c) 20, (d) 30, (e) 40, (f) 50, (g) 60 μg/mL ctDNA measured by PGE. (B) DPVs representing (a) the control of cQD-PGE in ABS, the guanine oxidation signal of (b) 10, (c) 20, (d) 30, (e) 40, (f) 50 μg/mL ctDNA measured by cQD-PGE. (C) Histograms representing the average guanine oxidation signal in the presence of ctDNA in different concentration varying between 2 and 50 μg/mL (n = 3).
The calibration plots representing the average guanine signals (n = 3) related to ctDNA immobilized onto the surface of PGE and cQD-PGE in varying concentrations from 2 to 10 μg/mL.
DNR concentration effect onto the sensor response for both PGE and cQD-PGE. The line graphs representing the average oxidation signal of DNR varying from 0.1 to 14 μM (n = 3).
The calibration plots representing the average DNR signals (n = 3) related to DNR immobilized PGE and cQD-PGE with in different DNR concentrations from 0 to 0.5 μM.
Histograms representing the average signals of DNR and guanine measured before and after interaction of DNR with ctDNA in different times (n = 3).

Nyquist diagrams recorded before and after interaction process during different interaction times. (a) cQD-PGE, (b) ctDNA immobilized cQD-PGE before interaction, (c) DNR immobilized cQD-PGE before interaction, (d) after interaction during (A) 3 min, (B) 5 min, (C) 10 min and (D) 15 min.

The average values (n = 4) of anodic and cathodic peak currents (Ia, Ic), relative anodic charge (Qa), relative cathodic charge (Qc), anodic and cathodic peak potentials (Epa, Epc) and ΔEp values for PGE and cQD-PGE.

The average oxidation signal of DNR and guanine (n = 3) measured before/after interaction performed in different times and the decrease ratios at both signals calculated after interaction of DNR and ctDNA in different interaction times.
The average Rct values (n = 3) before and after interaction occurred between DNR and DNA during 3 min, 5 min, 10 min and 15 min and the decrease % ratios.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: ⁎Submitted to special issue of HD52 meeting.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot modified electrodes developed for electrochemical monitoring of Daunorubicin-DNA interaction

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dot (cQD) modified disposable pencil graphite electrodes were developed for the first time in this study for electrochemical monitoring of drug-DNA interaction. The biomolecular interaction between calf thymus double stranded DNA (ctDNA) and an anthracycline antineoplastic drug, Daunorubicin (DNR) was investigated by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. For this purpose, experimental conditions, such as, the concentration of ctDNA, concentration of DNR and interaction time were optimized. Under optimum conditions, the detection limits of DNR and ctDNA were found to be 0.02 μg/mL and 0.89 μg/mL, respectively. The effect of interaction time between DNR and ctDNA was explored upon to the changes at both DNR and guanine oxidation signals. Drug-DNA interaction process was also examined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Daunorubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic drug that is isolated from Streptomyces peucetius and other varieties. It is used for treatment of several solid tumors, but this drug is mostly used for treatment of acute leukemia. Daunorubicin intercalates with DNA between its base pairs. Thus, this drug inhibits topoisomerase II activity in cell [1].

The interaction between drug and DNA has a key importance to understand for the development of new drugs. It occurs via different ways, such as intercalation, covalent binding, non-covalent binding, cross linking [2,3].

Detection of drug and DNA interaction is actualized via numerous methods. Some of these methods are chromatography, spectroscopy, optical and electrochemical methods. For instance, Meneghello et al. [4] used an anticancer drug mitoxantrone (MTX) as a model compound in order to demonstrate the possibility for performing electrochemical-SERS to present the redox behavior of a drug molecule bound to dsDNA. Traditional methods like chromatography and spectroscopy are time consuming and expensive on the other hand, electrochemical methods have many advantages over other methods; as, it provides selective, specific, cheap, fast detection protocol while being easy to use [5–10].

There have been many DNA based biosensors are developed with different transducers such as; electrochemical, optical, mass sensitive etc. [11]. Since the development of the nanotechnology, a lot of different nanomaterials used in biosensors, for example gold nanoparticles [12], quantum dots [13], carbon nanotubes [14], graphene [15], carbon dots [16] etc.

Carbon quantum dots (cQD) are the new member of carbon-based nanomaterials, generally in their sizes below the 10 nm [17]. These nanomaterials are very popular in the past decade due to their unique fluorescent properties, very good biocompatibility, many and cost-effective synthesis methods, low toxicity, water soluble, tunable sizes etc. [18]. Due to these advantages of cQD, they are used different areas such as; bioimaging, drug delivery, food safety, determination heavy metals in water, diagnosis, tumor marker detection, cancer cell analysis, phototherapy and radiotherapy, energy storage etc. [17,19–24]. Moreover, high surface to volume ratio and high biomolecule immobilization capacity have emerged the use of quantum dots (QDs) in targeted delivery and therapeutics. For the most of these specific applications, QDs are first conjugated with biomolecules such as nucleic acids, proteins or signaling molecules [25].

Electrochemical DNA biosensors are used in different areas such as; diagnosis [26], environmental monitoring [27], water analysis [28], pathogen detection [29], detection of DNA damage [30], detection of drug-DNA interaction [31], virus detection [32], bacteria detection [33], toxin detection [34] etc.

There have been the reports introducing the nanomaterials, polymers or nanocomposite modified or unmodified electrodes used for electrochemical detection of DNA-Daunorubicin interaction [35–42]. Saljooqi et al., [37] prepared Ag-4-ATP-MWCNT modified electrodes in order to detect DNA-Daunorubicin interaction by differential pulse voltammetry. In the one of the studies performed by our groups [35], chitosan modified PGEs were designed and applied for detection of DNA-Daunorubicin interaction by DPV. Cao et al. [43] developed 3D gold brush nanoelectrode ensembles (BNEEs) for the direct voltammetry of daunorubicin using square wave voltammetry technique.

To best of our knowledge, no report has been introduced yet in the literature presenting the cQD modified pencil graphite electrodes developed for electrochemical monitoring of the interaction of DNA-Daunorubicin. The quantum dots have been introduced in the sensor design to enhance the sensing performance of electrochemical DNA sensor. The surface modification of PGEs by using cQD was performed herein via passive adsorption technique. The microscopic and electrochemical characterization of cQD modified PGEs (cQD-PGEs) were performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. For monitoring of surface confined interaction between calf thymus double stranded DNA (ctDNA) and DNR, ctDNA was immobilized firstly onto the electrode surface and then, the electrochemical detection of DNA interaction with DNR was investigated voltammetrically. The effect of experimental parameters such as ctDNA concentration, DNR concentration with its interaction time was explored related to the electrode response. Moreover, EIS technique was also utilized for detection of the interaction between DNR and ctDNA in different interaction time.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of cQD were obtained using a Park XE7 instrument (Suwon, Korea) in tapping mode. The surface morphologies of the electrodes were acquired by scanning electron microscopy SEM (FEI QUANTA 250 FEG) coupled with EDX (Oxford Aztec). CV, DPV and EIS measurements were taken in a Faraday cage using Autolab-PGSTAT-302 electroanalysis system combined with NOVA 1.11 software package. Voltammetric raw data were treated using the Savitzky and Golay filter (level 2) of the GPES 4.9 software, followed by the moving average baseline correction with a peak width of 0.03. All measurements were performed with a traditional three-electrode system using a disposable pencil graphite electrode, a platinum wire and an Ag/AgCl/KCl/3 M (BAS, Model RE-5B, W. Lafayette, USA) as working, counter and reference electrode, respectively.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and they were supplied from global suppliers. Daunorubicin (DNR), calf thymus double stranded DNA (ctDNA), citric acid and hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Milli-Q ultra-pure water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions freshly.

ctDNA was dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer solution (TBS, pH 7.00) and kept frozen. Diluted solutions of ctDNA were prepared in 0.50 M acetate buffer solution containing 20 mM NaCl (ABS, pH 4.80). The stock solution of DNR was prepared in ultrapure distilled water. Diluted DNR solutions were prepared in 5 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.40).

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots was performed by a one-pot hydrothermal method according to our previously reported paper [45]. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid as carbon source was mixed with 55 mg of hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) as nitrogen source to obtain nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (cQD). Then, the mixture was put in a teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 240 °C for 5 h. The obtained cQD solution was purified using a dialysis bag.

ce:section.ce:section-title: cQD-PGE based DNA biosensor construction and drug-DNA interaction
cQD modification onto the surface of PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The working electrode, PGE surface was pretreated by applying a potential of +1.40 V for 30 s in 0.5 M acetate buffer solution (ABS, pH: 4.80) containing 20 mM NaCl. Each pretreated electrode was then modified with cQD by dipping PGEs into 100 μg/mL cQD in DMF during 1 h. After modification cQD-PGEs were air dried for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA immobilization onto the surface of cQD-PGEs
ce:section.ce:para: DNA biosensor was developed by immobilizing ctDNA onto electrode surface via passive adsorption by dipping cQD-PGEs into the 40 μg/mL ctDNA in ABS during 1 h. Each ctDNA immobilized cQD-PGEs were washed twice with ABS and non-specific adsorptions eliminated [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNR-ctDNA interaction onto the surface of cQD-PGEs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction step involved dipping the ctDNA immobilized cQD-PGEs into the 10 μM DNR solution in 5 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH: 7.40) during 15 min. Then each of the electrodes were washed with PBS.

The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used in order to evaluate the oxidation signal of both DNR and guanine in the same voltammetric scale in ABS (pH, 4.80). The oxidation signal of DNR was measured at the potential of +0.560 V where guanine oxidation signal was at +1.019 V. The interaction of DNR with ctDNA was evaluated by comparing the oxidation signals of both DNR and guanine before and after interaction. DPV measurements were performed between the potential of 0 and +1.40 V at a pulse amplitude of 50 mV and scan rate of 50 mV/s. CV measurements were performed in potential range from −0.50 V to +1.00 V with the scan rate as 50 mV/s in a redox probe solution of 2 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) prepared in 0.1 M KCl.

EIS technique was used for both characterization of cQD-PGEs and monitoring of drug-DNA interaction. The impedance was acquired in 2.5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) prepared in 0.1 M KCl redox probe solution by scanning the frequency range from 105 Hz to 10−1 Hz in an open-circuit potential value of +0.23 V (vs Ag/AgCl/3 M) with a sinusoidal signal of 10 mV. The frequency interval was divided into 98 logarithmically equidistant measure points. The Randles circuit was used as equivalent circuit model used for fitting of the impedance data. The elements of Randles circuit values were calculated using the fitting program AUTOLAB 302, NOVA software package (version 1.11 Eco Chemie, The Netherlands). It was comprised of the solution resistance (Rs), the double layer capacitance (C), the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and Warburg impedance (W). The respective semicircle diameter corresponds to the charge–transfer resistance, Rct.

In our previous study [45], the detailed characterization of cQD was reported. Therefore, herein, AFM technique was performed in order to confirm the presence of quantum dots. Fig. 1 shows the obtained lateral and 3D AFM images and the corresponding height profile of well-dispersed cQD on a freshly cleaned silicon substrate. A line taken from the image reveals that cQD has similar distribution with a height of 2–4 nm (Fig. 1c).

The morphological characterization of the prepared PGE and cQD-PGE was performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2A and B respectively show the SEM micrographs for PGE and cQD-PGE at low and high magnification, which show an increased porosity after modification of cQD onto PGE surface. In order to verify the modification of the uniform modification of cQD onto PGE surface EDX analysis was also performed. Fig. S1 and S2 show EDX spectra of SEM images for PGE and cQD-PGE, respectively. Table S1 and Table S2 show the EDX analysis results, which proves the presence of nitrogen (0.18%) after modification of cQD onto PGE surface.

The electrochemical behavior of cQD-PGEs was also explored in comparison to unmodified PGEs by using CV technique in 0.1 M KCl containing 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. The obtained CV curves given in Fig. 3 demonstrates that the cQD were absorbed onto the surface of the PGEs successfully. The corresponding anodic and cathodic currents; Ia, Ic with the relative charge; Q (mC), and anodic and cathodic peak potentials Epa, Epc (mV) and ΔEp values for PGE and cQD-PGE are shown in Table 1 . In this point, the slightly inhibiting electron transfers between the cQD-PGEs and the electro-active anions ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion which is caused by relatively negative charged surface of cQD [47–49].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the resulting cQD-PGE could also give detail information about the modification process. For this purpose, EIS measurements were performed in the absence/in the presence of cQD modification. Since the diameter of the semicircle at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron-transfer resistance (Rct), the change at Rct was associated with the change at the surface characteristics occurred after the surface modification by cQD (Fig. 3-II). The average Rct values were 91.97 ± 17.02 Ohm (RSD %, 18%, n = 3) and 252.68 ± 26.02 Ohm (RSD %, 10%, n = 3) for PGE and cQD-PGE, respectively. Accordingly, 2.7-fold increase was recorded at Rct value after modification of cQD onto the PGE surface. Since these results in good agreement with other some recent studies of the thiol graphene quantum dots [47], it can be concluded that EIS and CV results were found in a good correlation. Additionally, the apparent fractional coverage values (Q
IS
R ) were calculated for 100 μg/mL ctDNA immobilized PGE and cQD-PGE according to Eq. (1) described by Janek et al. [50] and found to be 0.718 and 0.737, respectively. The related Nyquist plots were shown in Fig. S3. The higher Q
IS
R value was indicated that successful modification and the more coverage was obtained at the surface of cQD-PGE in comparison to the PGE. This result was a proof that cQD enlarged the surface area as a result of the behavior of heteroatom-containing cQD for more binding of nucleic acids.
(1)
Q IS R
= 1 −
R ct
cQD − PGE / PGE
R ct
ctDNA
cQD − PGE / PGE

To optimize ctDNA concentration, the electrodes were immersed into DNA solution in different concentrations varying from 2 to 60 μg/mL during 1 h and accordingly, the oxidation signal of guanine was measured by DPV (Fig. 4 ). An increase was recorded at biosensor response when there was an increase at DNA concentration. Similarly, an enhancement at guanine signal in the case of cQD modification onto PGE surface in comparison to the PGE. The highest guanine oxidation signal was measured as 6.68 ± 0.51 μA in the presence of 40 μg/mL ctDNA via cQD-PGE whereas it was measured as 6.11 ± 0.54 μA by PGE. Thus, the optimum ctDNA concentration was chosen as 40 μg/mL for the preparation of DNA modified cQD-PGE.

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the equation reported by Miller and Miller [44]; LOD = (3 x SD)/(Slope of calibration curve, Fig. 5 inset) where SD denotes the standard deviation calculated by regression analysis for S/N = 3 with the Equations I (μA) = 0.24C ctDNA (μg/mL) − 0.18, R
2 = 0.98 for PGE, I (μA) = 0.30C ctDNA (μg/mL) + 0.02, R
2 = 0.99 for cQD-PGE.

The LODs were calculated for PGE and cQD-PGE in the concentration range between 2 and 10 μg/mL ctDNA and found to be respectively, 1.17 μg/mL and 0.89 μg/mL. A 24% lower DL is obtained by cQD-PGE in comparison to the one calculated by PGE.

Next, the effect of DNR concentration upon electrode response was examined by using PGE and cQD-PGE. Under this aim, both electrodes were immersed into DNR solution in different concentrations from 0.1 to 14 μM during 15 min, and accordingly, the oxidation signal of DNR was shown in Fig. 6 . There was an increase at DNR signal by cQD-PGE for each DNR concentration in contrast to the one measured by PGE. The highest oxidation signal of DNR was measured as 24.27 ± 2.84 μA in the presence of 10 μM DNR by cQD-PGE, however it was measured as 22.32 ± 1.80 μA by PGE. Hence, the optimum DNR concentration was chosen as 10 μM furtherly to perform surface confined interaction studies via cQD-PGE.

The LODs were calculated for PGE and cQD-PGE in the concentration range between 0.1 and 0.5 μM DNR and found to be 66 nM (equals to 0.03 μg/mL) and 37 nM (equals to 0.02 μg/mL), respectively. A 44% lower DL was obtained by cQD-PGE in comparison to the one calculated by PGE. Additionally, the LODs were lower than the earlier reports related to DNR detection [35,36,42] and greatly lower than IC50 value which was reported as 136.80 μM [51]. In addition, the sensitivity of the PGE and cQD-PGE was calculated from the ratio of the slopes of the calibration plots shown in Fig. 7 , and the active surface area of the working electrodes. The sensitivity values were found respectively to be 38.52 μA μM−1 cm−2 and 39.43 μA μM−1 cm−2 for PGE and cQD-PGE.

Next, the surface-confined interaction occurred between DNR and ctDNA at the surface of cQD-PGE was explored. Firstly, the effect of interaction time was investigated in different time from 3 to 15 min. Before and after interaction, both oxidation signals of DNR and guanine were measured respectively at the potential of +0.56 V and +1.01 V in the same voltammetric scale (Fig. S4).

The carbon quantum dot modified single-use graphite electrodes, cQD-PGEs, were used for the first time herein in order to detect the interaction of DNR with ctDNA based on the changes at the oxidation signal of DNR and guanine. The results of voltammetric detection of the interaction between DNR and ctDNA were also confirmed by EIS technique.

Our assay enabled a sensitive, time-saving and practical detection protocol for detection of interaction between nucleic acid and anticancer drug in comparison to earlier reports in the literature [6,7,37,41,52–56].

Total assay was completed in 2 h and 15 min. The LODs were calculated for PGE and cQD-PGE in the concentration range of 0.1–0.5 μM DNR and found to be 66 nM and 37 nM, respectively. Additionally, the LODs were lower than the earlier reports related to DNR detection [35,36,40,42] and greatly lower than IC50 value which was reported as 136.80 μM [51]. The sensitivity values were found to be 38.52 μA μM−1 cm−2 and 39.43 μA μM−1 cm−2 for PGE and cQD-PGE, respectively. Consequently, the utility of single-use cQD modified electrodes could provide a simple, rapid detection and might have a promising future in point-of-care tests related to nucleic acids.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Ece Eksin:Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Huseyin Senturk:Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Erhan Zor:Formal analysis, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Haluk Bingol:Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing.Arzum Erdem:Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.E. would like to express her gratitude to the Turkish Academy of Sciences (TUBA) as a Principal member for its partial support.
